title: Schiacciata all'uva
subtitle: Florentine harvest flatbread pressed with red wine grapes
excerpt: A Florentine harvest flatbread made by pressing fresh wine grapes into a dimpled olive oil dough and scattering sugar and rosemary over the top. Traditionally baked in October when the Sangiovese harvest is in, it sits between focaccia and grape cake: oily and crisp underneath, sweet and sticky on top where the grapes have burst and caramelised. Eaten warm as a snack or dessert.
sourceNotes: Schiacciata all'uva is a Florentine seasonal bread tied to the October grape harvest and documented in Tuscan culinary literature throughout the twentieth century. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, freely available at Project Gutenberg IT), describes Tuscan grape-based baked goods of this tradition. The flatbread is described in multiple post-1928 Tuscan cookbooks as a harvest preparation using fresh wine grapes pressed into an oiled dough.

content.text: Schiacciata all'uva is only worth making with flavourful grapes. The small Sangiovese or Concord types used in Tuscany in October are ideal; seedless red grapes sold year-round work if they have genuine sweetness. The technique is the same as focaccia, with one difference: the
grapes are pressed firmly into the proved dough
rather than laid on top, so they sink into the crumb and burst during baking, releasing their juice into the interior.

items.name: Grapes
items.prepNote: small red wine grapes or seedless red grapes, left whole

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, salt, and 40 g caster sugar in a large bowl, keeping the salt and yeast on opposite sides before mixing. Pour in the warm water and olive oil and mix to a rough, slightly sticky dough. Turn out and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth. Return to a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave in a warm place for 60 minutes until doubled.

Pour 3 tablespoons of olive oil into a 30 x 40 cm rimmed tin. Tip in the dough and press it gently toward the edges. If it springs back, leave uncovered for 10 minutes to relax, then press again. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes until puffed.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Press the grapes firmly into the proved dough all over, pushing them in to about half their depth. Scatter the rosemary leaves across the surface and sprinkle with the remaining 2 tablespoons of caster sugar. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until deep golden at the edges and the grapes have burst and caramelised. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes before cutting. Eat warm.
